asset_name: MOTS-c Exercise and Age-Related Physical Decline
status: CLOSED FOR CORE PRIMARY FULL-TEXT EXTRACTION

publication.title: MOTS-c is an exercise-induced mitochondrial-encoded regulator of age-dependent physical decline and muscle homeostasis

study_design: Open-access translational study combining human exercise sampling, mouse intervention experiments and cell models.
population_or_model: Ten healthy young men in the exercise-expression component; young, middle-aged and old mice; C2C12 cells.
intervention_or_exposure: Human exercise sampling measured endogenous MOTS-c. Exogenous MOTS-c intervention outcomes were tested primarily in mice and cells.
outcomes: In the ten-man human exercise component, skeletal-muscle MOTS-c rose 11.9-fold after exercise and circulating levels rose about 1.5–1.6-fold during/after exercise.
Exogenous MOTS-c improved multiple physical-performance outcomes in mouse models.
Old treated mice ran approximately twofold longer and 2.16-fold farther in one reported experiment.
Late-life intermittent treatment improved several healthspan measures in mice.
Overall lifespan results were not statistically conclusive; the overall curve was reported as p=0.23.
The human component measured endogenous response, not therapeutic efficacy of injected MOTS-c.
limitations: Human sample was small and observational with respect to endogenous exercise response.
Treatment efficacy was established in mice, not humans.
Multiple animal doses and models do not create a human protocol.
permitted_claims: Exercise increased endogenous MOTS-c in a small human study.
Exogenous MOTS-c improved physical-capacity outcomes in several mouse models.
The study did not establish injected MOTS-c efficacy in humans.
prohibited_claims: Human exercise-performance enhancement claims from injections.
Conversion of mouse regimens into human dosing.
source_closure: PRIMARY FULL-TEXT WEB EXTRACTION COMPLETED FOR CORE CLAIMS
